---
title: "Climate Change"
description: "How Canada's climate has warmed — the national record, the faster-warming North, and long-run temperatures for recognizable cities"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import os
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
```

Canada is warming about twice as fast as the world as a whole, and its North faster
still. The temperatures here are drawn from Environment and Climate Change Canada's
homogenized climate records — adjusted for station moves, instrument changes, and
other non-climatic breaks so that long trends are comparable — and are expressed,
where relevant, as departures from the **1961–1990 reference period** (the standard
baseline). They describe observed change; they are not projections.

## A warming country

The national average temperature has risen markedly since records began in 1948.
Each bar is one year's departure from the 1961–1990 average — red above it, blue
below. The early decades are mostly blue; recent years are almost entirely red, and
**2024 was the warmest year on record** at about +3.1 °C above the reference period.

```{python}
#| echo: false
temp_path = "data/environment/cesi_temperature.csv"
if os.path.exists(temp_path):
    t = pd.read_csv(temp_path)
    colors = [CANADA_COLOR if v >= 0 else "#1f77b4" for v in t["departure_c"]]
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
        x=t["year"], y=t["departure_c"], marker_color=colors,
        hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:+.1f} °C vs 1961–1990<extra></extra>"))
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, bargap=0.15,
        yaxis=dict(title="Departure from 1961–1990 average (°C)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   ticksuffix=" °C", zeroline=False),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", dtick=10),
        margin=dict(t=30, b=120),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Source: ECCC, Canadian Environmental Sustainability Indicators | Data as of: {get_data_date(temp_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.20, showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Temperature data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Mapped: the temperature, and how much it has warmed

The national average hides the geography. These continuous maps fill it in, drawn from
**ERA5-Land** — the European Centre's high-resolution (~9 km) reanalysis, which blends weather
observations with a physical model into a gap-free temperature field over the land (oceans and
large lakes are left blank). The first map shows the **typical temperature for each month**
(the 1991–2020 normal) — pick a month to see what it's usually like where you live. Then: **how the most
recent months compare to that normal** (has it been unusual lately?), and the **change since the mid-century**.
Hover anywhere for the value at that spot.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json, base64, datetime
from IPython.display import display, HTML
from pipeline.charts import relief_map

_man_p = "data/geo/era5_climate_manifest.json"
_pts_p = "data/geo/era5_climate_points.json"
_pts = json.load(open(_pts_p)) if os.path.exists(_pts_p) else {}
_CFG = {"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
# CSS gradients matching the WebP colour scales (ColorBrewer RdYlBu / RdBu, reversed) — kept
# inline so the render needs no matplotlib (it's a builder-only dep, not in requirements.txt).
_CMAP_CSS = {
    "RdYlBu_r": "#313695,#4575b4,#74add1,#abd9e9,#e0f3f8,#ffffbf,#fee090,#fdae61,#f46d43,#d73027,#a50026",
    "RdBu_r": "#053061,#2166ac,#4393c3,#92c5de,#d1e5f0,#f7f7f7,#fddbc7,#f4a582,#d6604d,#b2182b,#67001f"}

def _uri(webp):
    return "data:image/webp;base64," + base64.b64encode(open(f"data/geo/{webp}", "rb").read()).decode()

def _legend(title, cmap, vmin, vmax, diverging):
    mid = "0 °C" if diverging else f"{(vmin+vmax)/2:+.0f} °C"
    return HTML(f'<div style="max-width:480px;margin:-4px 0 18px;font-size:12px;color:#555">'
        f'<div style="font-weight:600;margin-bottom:3px">{title}</div>'
        f'<div style="height:12px;border-radius:3px;background:linear-gradient(to right,{_CMAP_CSS[cmap]})"></div>'
        f'<div style="display:flex;justify-content:space-between"><span>{vmin:+.0f} °C</span>'
        f'<span>{mid}</span><span>{vmax:+.0f} °C</span></div></div>')

def _climate_map(key, title, src, diverging=False, hover_label=None):
    man = json.load(open(_man_p)); prov = json.load(open("data/geo/canada_provinces.geojson"))
    L = next(l for l in man["layers"] if l["key"] == key)
    vals = (_pts.get("vals") or {}).get(key)
    fig = relief_map(_uri(L["webp"]), L["corners"], boundary_geojson=prov, zoom=2.2,
                     source_note=f"{src} {man['attribution']}.",
                     hover_lon=_pts.get("lon"), hover_lat=_pts.get("lat"), hover_val=vals,
                     hover_label=hover_label or ("Warming" if diverging else "Average temperature"),
                     hover_fmt="+.1f" if diverging else ".1f", hover_suffix=" °C")
    fig.show(config=_CFG)
    display(_legend(title, L["cmap"], L["vmin"], L["vmax"], diverging))

def _month_map(src):
    """Monthly-normal temperature map with a month selector (defaults to the current month).
    The dropdown swaps the image layer's source + the hover values; all months share one scale,
    so the legend is fixed and flipping months reads the seasonal swing as colour."""
    man = json.load(open(_man_p)); prov = json.load(open("data/geo/canada_provinces.geojson"))
    months = [l for l in man["layers"] if l.get("kind") == "month"]
    di = next((i for i, l in enumerate(months) if l["month"] == datetime.date.today().month), 0)
    vals = _pts.get("vals") or {}
    L0 = months[di]
    fig = relief_map(_uri(L0["webp"]), L0["corners"], boundary_geojson=prov, zoom=2.2,
                     source_note=f"{src} {man['attribution']}.",
                     hover_lon=_pts.get("lon"), hover_lat=_pts.get("lat"), hover_val=vals.get(L0["key"]),
                     hover_label="Average temperature", hover_fmt=".1f", hover_suffix=" °C")
    btns = [dict(label=l["label"], method="update",
                 args=[{"customdata": [vals.get(l["key"])]},
                       {"mapbox.layers[1].source": _uri(l["webp"])}]) for l in months]
    menu = dict(type="dropdown", direction="down", active=di, showactive=True,
                x=0.01, y=0.99, xanchor="left", yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                borderwidth=1, font=dict(size=12), pad=dict(t=2, b=2, l=4, r=4), buttons=btns)
    fig.update_layout(updatemenus=list(fig.layout.updatemenus) + [menu])
    fig.show(config=_CFG)
    display(_legend("Average temperature by month (1991–2020 normal)", L0["cmap"], L0["vmin"], L0["vmax"], False))

def _recent_map(src):
    """Recent-anomaly map (latest 1 / 3 / 12 months vs the 1991–2020 normal) with a window selector,
    opening on the past month. Shared diverging scale, so the fixed legend reads across all windows."""
    man = json.load(open(_man_p)); prov = json.load(open("data/geo/canada_provinces.geojson"))
    rec = [l for l in man["layers"] if l.get("kind") == "recent"]
    vals = _pts.get("vals") or {}
    L0 = rec[0]
    fig = relief_map(_uri(L0["webp"]), L0["corners"], boundary_geojson=prov, zoom=2.2,
                     source_note=f"{src} {man['attribution']}.",
                     hover_lon=_pts.get("lon"), hover_lat=_pts.get("lat"), hover_val=vals.get(L0["key"]),
                     hover_label="vs 1991–2020 normal", hover_fmt="+.1f", hover_suffix=" °C")
    btns = [dict(label=f'{l["label"]} ({l["span"]})', method="update",
                 args=[{"customdata": [vals.get(l["key"])]},
                       {"mapbox.layers[1].source": _uri(l["webp"])}]) for l in rec]
    menu = dict(type="dropdown", direction="down", active=0, showactive=True,
                x=0.01, y=0.99, xanchor="left", yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                borderwidth=1, font=dict(size=12), pad=dict(t=2, b=2, l=4, r=4), buttons=btns)
    fig.update_layout(updatemenus=list(fig.layout.updatemenus) + [menu])
    fig.show(config=_CFG)
    display(_legend("Temperature vs the 1991–2020 average (warmer = red, cooler = blue)",
                    L0["cmap"], L0["vmin"], L0["vmax"], True))
```

### Typical temperature, month by month

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(_man_p):
    _month_map("Source: ECMWF Copernicus Climate Change Service — ERA5-Land monthly means, 1991–2020 normal.")
else:
    print("Climate map not yet available — run python -m pipeline.build_era5_climate.")
```

### Has it been unusual lately?

How the most recent months compare to the **1991–2020 average** — red where it has run warmer than normal,
blue where cooler. Switch the window between the past month, three months, and year, and hover for the value
where you live. A single warm or cool month is **weather**, not a trend; for the long-term signal, see the
warming map below.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(_man_p):
    _recent_map("Source: ECMWF Copernicus Climate Change Service — ERA5-Land monthly means vs the 1991–2020 normal.")
```

The map is a snapshot; the run of recent months shows whether it is a one-off or a pattern — the
Canada-wide average for each of the last two years:

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
_rec_p = "data/geo/era5_recent_anomaly.csv"
if os.path.exists(_rec_p):
    rs = pd.read_csv(_rec_p)
    rs["date"] = pd.to_datetime(rs["month"] + "-01")
    bar_colors = ["#c0392b" if v >= 0 else "#2c7fb8" for v in rs["anomaly"]]
    figs = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=rs["date"], y=rs["anomaly"], marker_color=bar_colors,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%b %Y}</b>: %{y:+.1f} °C vs normal<extra></extra>"))
    figs.add_hline(y=0, line_width=1, line_color="#888")
    figs.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=300, margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=44),
        yaxis_title="°C vs 1991–2020 average", showlegend=False,
        xaxis=dict(showgrid=False), yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#eee", zeroline=False))
    figs.add_annotation(text="Source: ECMWF Copernicus Climate Change Service — ERA5-Land.",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.26, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#888"), align="left")
    figs.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

### How much it has warmed

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(_man_p):
    _climate_map("warming_annual", "Change in annual temperature, 2016–2025 vs 1961–1990",
        "Source: ECMWF Copernicus Climate Change Service — ERA5-Land, 2016–2025 minus 1961–1990.", diverging=True)
```

## The North is warming fastest

Warming is not even across the country. The bars below are Environment and Climate
Change Canada's **11 climate regions** — broad areas covering the whole country, each
shown as the *average* of its weather stations (homogenized, then interpolated to a
grid and averaged over the region), not a single location. Over 1948–2024 the
far-northern regions have warmed roughly twice as fast as the southern and Atlantic
ones — the pattern known as **Arctic amplification**, driven partly by the loss of
reflective snow and sea ice. The fastest-warming areas are the **Mackenzie Valley**
(the forested Northwest Territories), **Yukon and northern B.C.**, and the **mainland
Arctic tundra**; the Pacific coast and Atlantic Canada have warmed least. The dashed
line is the national average; ECCC's [Climate Trends and Variations
Bulletin](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/climate-change/science-research-data/climate-trends-variability/trends-variations.html)
maps the exact region boundaries.

```{python}
#| echo: false
ctvb_path = "data/environment/cesi_ctvb_regional.csv"
if os.path.exists(ctvb_path):
    c = pd.read_csv(ctvb_path)
    # ECCC's terse region names -> clearer general-audience labels; the official
    # ECCC name is kept for the hover (traceability to the source bulletin).
    REGION_LABELS = {
        "Mackenzie": "Mackenzie Valley (N.W.T.)",
        "North BC/Yukon": "Yukon & northern B.C.",
        "Arctic Tundra": "Mainland Arctic tundra",
        "Arctic Mountains": "Arctic islands (Baffin/Ellesmere)",
        "North Western": "Western boreal forest",
        "North Eastern": "Eastern boreal (Qué.–Labrador)",
        "South BC": "Southern B.C. mountains",
        "Pacific": "Pacific coast (B.C.)",
        "Great Lakes": "Great Lakes–St. Lawrence",
        "Prairies": "Prairies",
        "Atlantic Canada": "Atlantic Canada",
    }
    REGION_OFFICIAL = {
        "Mackenzie": "Mackenzie District", "North BC/Yukon": "Yukon/North B.C. Mountains",
        "Arctic Tundra": "Arctic Tundra", "Arctic Mountains": "Arctic Mountains and Fiords",
        "North Western": "Northwestern Forest", "North Eastern": "Northeastern Forest",
        "South BC": "South B.C. Mountains", "Pacific": "Pacific Coast",
        "Great Lakes": "Great Lakes/St. Lawrence", "Prairies": "Prairies",
        "Atlantic Canada": "Atlantic Canada",
    }
    canada = c.loc[c["region"] == "Canada", "trend_c"]
    canada_v = float(canada.iloc[0]) if len(canada) else None
    regions = c[c["region"] != "Canada"].sort_values("trend_c").copy()
    regions["label"] = regions["region"].map(REGION_LABELS).fillna(regions["region"])
    regions["official"] = regions["region"].map(REGION_OFFICIAL).fillna(regions["region"])
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
        y=regions["label"], x=regions["trend_c"], orientation="h",
        marker=dict(color=regions["trend_c"], colorscale="Reds", cmin=0, showscale=False),
        customdata=regions["official"],
        hovertemplate="%{y}: +%{x:.1f} °C since 1948<br>ECCC climate region: %{customdata}<extra></extra>"))
    if canada_v is not None:
        fig.add_vline(x=canada_v, line_dash="dash", line_color="#555",
                      annotation_text=f"Canada +{canada_v:.1f} °C",
                      annotation_position="top")
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=480,
        xaxis=dict(title="Warming trend, 1948–2024 (°C)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   ticksuffix=" °C", rangemode="tozero"),
        yaxis=dict(title="", tickfont=dict(size=11), automargin=True),
        margin=dict(t=30, b=90, l=10),
        annotations=[dict(
            text="Source: ECCC Climate Trends and Variations Bulletin | 1948–2024 trend",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.16, showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Regional trend data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

### Where the regions are

Each marker sits at a region's **approximate centre** (these are areas, not points),
coloured by the same warming rate as the bars above — the warm reds cluster across
the North. Hover for the rate; the bar chart above has the exact figures.

```{python}
#| echo: false
ctvb_path = "data/environment/cesi_ctvb_regional.csv"
if os.path.exists(ctvb_path):
    # Representative centre point per region (lat, lon, short label). APPROXIMATE
    # locations read off ECCC's region map — markers, NOT exact boundaries.
    REGION_POINTS = {
        "Pacific": (52.5, -128.0, "Pacific coast"),
        "South BC": (49.5, -118.0, "Southern BC"),
        "North BC/Yukon": (61.0, -134.0, "Yukon & N. BC"),
        "Mackenzie": (64.0, -121.0, "Mackenzie Valley"),
        "North Western": (56.5, -106.0, "Western boreal"),
        "North Eastern": (52.0, -73.0, "Eastern boreal"),
        "Prairies": (50.0, -105.0, "Prairies"),
        "Great Lakes": (45.0, -80.0, "Great Lakes"),
        "Atlantic Canada": (47.0, -62.0, "Atlantic"),
        "Arctic Tundra": (66.5, -98.0, "Arctic tundra"),
        "Arctic Mountains": (74.0, -82.0, "Arctic islands"),
    }
    cm = pd.read_csv(ctvb_path)
    cm = cm[cm["region"].isin(REGION_POINTS)].copy()
    cm["lat"] = cm["region"].map(lambda r: REGION_POINTS[r][0])
    cm["lon"] = cm["region"].map(lambda r: REGION_POINTS[r][1])
    cm["lbl"] = cm["region"].map(lambda r: REGION_POINTS[r][2])
    fig = go.Figure(go.Scattermapbox(
        lat=cm["lat"], lon=cm["lon"], mode="markers+text",
        marker=dict(size=16, color=cm["trend_c"], colorscale="Reds",
                    cmin=0, cmax=float(cm["trend_c"].max()), showscale=True,
                    colorbar=dict(title="Warming<br>1948–2024", ticksuffix=" °C", thickness=12)),
        text=cm["lbl"], textposition="top center", textfont=dict(size=9, color="#333"),
        customdata=cm["trend_c"],
        hovertemplate="%{text}: +%{customdata:.1f} °C since 1948<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(
        mapbox=dict(style="carto-positron", center=dict(lat=64, lon=-96), zoom=1.9),
        height=430, margin=dict(t=10, b=34, l=0, r=0),
        annotations=[dict(
            text="Source: ECCC Climate Trends and Variations Bulletin",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.07, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Regional trend data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Long-run temperatures, city by city

Some Canadian temperature records are remarkably long: Toronto's reaches back to
**1841**. Use the dropdown to switch cities. Each chart shows two series that are
deliberately kept **separate, not spliced**: the **homogenized** record (the
trend-quality series, which the open national dataset currently carries to 2020) and
a **raw** recent tail computed from current daily observations.

The Arctic stations tell their own story. **Alert and Eureka begin only in 1948 and
1951** — there are no earlier instrumental records that far north, because the first
permanent High Arctic weather stations were not built until the 1947–1950 Joint
Arctic Weather Stations program.

```{python}
#| echo: false
city_path = "data/environment/city_temperatures.csv"
if os.path.exists(city_path):
    import numpy as np
    ct = pd.read_csv(city_path)
    cities = ["Toronto", "Vancouver", "Winnipeg", "Calgary", "Alert", "Eureka"]
    cities = [c for c in cities if c in set(ct["city"])]
    fig = go.Figure()
    trace_city = []   # 2 traces per city: homogenized, raw tail
    for city in cities:
        homog = ct[(ct["city"] == city) & (ct["series"] == "Homogenized (AHCCD)")].sort_values("year")
        raw = ct[(ct["city"] == city) & (ct["series"] == "Recent (raw)")].sort_values("year")
        vis = (city == cities[0])
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=homog["year"], y=homog["temp"], name="Homogenized record (to 2020)",
            mode="lines", line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=2), visible=vis,
            hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.1f} °C<extra>Homogenized</extra>"))
        trace_city.append(city)
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=raw["year"], y=raw["temp"], name="Recent observations (raw)",
            mode="lines+markers", line=dict(color="#ff7f0e", width=2, dash="dot"),
            marker=dict(size=5), visible=vis,
            hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.1f} °C<extra>Recent (raw)</extra>"))
        trace_city.append(city)
    buttons = [dict(method="update", label=city,
                    args=[{"visible": [tc == city for tc in trace_city]},
                          {"xaxis.autorange": True, "yaxis.autorange": True}])
               for city in cities]
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=520, hovermode="x unified",
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.07, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        yaxis=dict(title="Annual mean temperature (°C)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix=" °C"),
        # legend on the right so the range slider owns the bottom (project idiom)
        legend=dict(orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1, xanchor="left", x=1.02),
        # dropdown top-LEFT, clear of the modebar (top-right) and the right edge
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, active=0, x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.12,
                          yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        margin=dict(t=50, b=120, r=180),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Source: ECCC AHCCD (homogenized) + MSC daily observations | Data as of: {get_data_date(city_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.42, showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("City temperature data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Warming by season

Warming isn't spread evenly across the year. In much of the country the **cold
seasons have warmed most** — winter in the continental interior (Winnipeg, Calgary),
and autumn in the High Arctic, where the later autumn freeze-up of sea ice keeps the
season milder for longer. Cities near the coasts and the Great Lakes (Toronto,
Vancouver) warm more evenly across the seasons. Each season's warming rate over its
homogenized record (°C per century) is shown in the legend, for either the seasonal
**average** temperature or the average **daytime high**. The daytime high has
generally warmed a little less than the overall average — because the fastest
warming has come at night.

```{python}
#| echo: false
seas_path = "data/environment/city_temperatures_seasonal.csv"
if os.path.exists(seas_path):
    import numpy as np
    cs = pd.read_csv(seas_path)
    cities = [c for c in ["Toronto", "Vancouver", "Winnipeg", "Calgary", "Alert", "Eureka"]
              if c in set(cs["city"])]
    SEASON_COLORS = {"Winter": "#1f77b4", "Spring": "#2ca02c",
                     "Summer": "#C0392B", "Autumn": "#ff7f0e"}
    SEASONS = ["Winter", "Spring", "Summer", "Autumn"]
    # Two metrics share one chart via a second dropdown: the seasonal average
    # temperature and the seasonal average of daily-high temperatures.
    METRICS = [("temp", "Average temperature", "Seasonal mean temperature (°C)"),
               ("temp_max", "Daytime highs", "Seasonal mean daily-high temperature (°C)")]
    fig = go.Figure()
    trace_city = []
    ys = {m[0]: [] for m in METRICS}      # per-metric y arrays, one per trace
    names = {m[0]: [] for m in METRICS}   # per-metric legend names, one per trace
    for city in cities:
        for season in SEASONS:
            s = cs[(cs["city"] == city) & (cs["season"] == season)].sort_values("year")
            x = s["year"].tolist()
            for col, _lbl, _yt in METRICS:
                nm = season
                sv = s.dropna(subset=[col])
                if len(sv) >= 10:
                    slope = np.polyfit(sv["year"].to_numpy(float), sv[col].to_numpy(float), 1)[0]
                    nm = f"{season} ({slope * 100:+.1f} °C/century)"
                ys[col].append(s[col].tolist())
                names[col].append(nm)
            fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                x=x, y=s["temp"].tolist(), name=names["temp"][-1], mode="lines",
                line=dict(color=SEASON_COLORS[season], width=1.8),
                visible=(city == cities[0]),
                hovertemplate=f"{season} %{{x}}: %{{y:.1f}} °C<extra></extra>"))
            trace_city.append(city)
    city_buttons = [dict(method="update", label=city,
                    args=[{"visible": [tc == city for tc in trace_city]},
                          {"xaxis.autorange": True, "yaxis.autorange": True}])
               for city in cities]
    metric_buttons = [dict(method="update", label=lbl,
                    args=[{"y": ys[col], "name": names[col]},
                          {"yaxis.title.text": yt, "yaxis.autorange": True}])
               for col, lbl, yt in METRICS]
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=520, hovermode="x unified",
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.07, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        yaxis=dict(title="Seasonal mean temperature (°C)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix=" °C"),
        legend=dict(orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1, xanchor="left", x=1.02),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=city_buttons, active=0, x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.22,
                          yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True),
                     dict(buttons=metric_buttons, active=0, x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.11,
                          yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        margin=dict(t=85, b=120, r=205),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Source: ECCC AHCCD seasonal (homogenized, to 2020), via the GeoMet API | Data as of: {get_data_date(seas_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.42, showarrow=False,
            font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Seasonal temperature data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Warming spirals: south vs. Arctic

The famous climate spirals (NASA, Ed Hawkins) keep every month of data and still
look smooth because they average the whole globe. A single station can't be that
smooth — so these two, **Toronto** and **Eureka** on the **same scale**, keep the
full **month-to-month** detail and stay deliberately jagged. Each month sits at its
position around the circle; the further out, the further above the 1961–1990 average
(the green ring is that normal; faint rings every 4 °C). Each year is one coloured
loop — blue if the year ran below normal, red if above. Press ▶ to build either up
year by year — **Eureka's loops swing far wider and redden far harder than
Toronto's: Arctic amplification you can see.**

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
mon_path = "data/environment/city_temperatures_monthly.csv"
MON_S = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
         "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
_spiral = {}
_rmin_all, _rmax_all = 0.0, 0.0
if os.path.exists(mon_path):
    _mdf = pd.read_csv(mon_path)
    for _city in ["Toronto", "Eureka"]:
        dd = _mdf[_mdf["city"] == _city].copy()
        if dd.empty:
            continue
        _b = dd[(dd["year"] >= 1961) & (dd["year"] <= 1990)].groupby("month")["temp"].mean()
        dd = dd[dd["month"].isin(_b.index)].copy()
        dd["anom"] = dd["temp"] - dd["month"].map(_b)
        dd = dd.sort_values(["year", "month"]).reset_index(drop=True)
        dd["anom_s"] = dd["anom"]   # RAW monthly anomaly (no smoothing) — keeps full month-to-month detail
        dd = dd[dd["year"] >= 1948].reset_index(drop=True)   # common era (Arctic stations begin ~1948)
        dd["theta"] = (dd["month"] - 1) / 12 * 360
        _spiral[_city] = dd
        _rmin_all = min(_rmin_all, float(dd["anom_s"].min()))
        _rmax_all = max(_rmax_all, float(dd["anom_s"].max()))

_RR = [float(np.floor(_rmin_all)), float(np.ceil(_rmax_all))]   # SHARED radial scale
_TICKS = [v for v in [-12, -8, -4, 0, 4, 8, 12] if _RR[0] <= v <= _RR[1]]
_TTXT = ["0" if v == 0 else (f"+{int(v)}°" if v > 0 else f"{int(v)}°") for v in _TICKS]
_CMAX = max(2.0, _rmax_all)

import plotly.colors as pc
# Colour clamp (°C) for each year-line's colour. Tied to yearly-MEAN anomalies
# (~±2.5 for a southern city), NOT single-month extremes, so the blue<->red
# gradient is vivid in both panels; the Arctic's hottest years saturate to red
# (their greater magnitude still shows via the radius / outer rings).
_CMAX_LINE = 2.5
def _col(anom):
    t = (anom + _CMAX_LINE) / (2 * _CMAX_LINE)   # -clamp -> blue, 0 -> white, +clamp -> red
    return pc.sample_colorscale("RdBu_r", min(1.0, max(0.0, t)))[0]


def show_spiral(city, title):
    if city not in _spiral:
        print(f"{city} monthly data not available — run the pipeline.")
        return
    dd = _spiral[city].reset_index(drop=True)
    years = sorted(dd["year"].unique())
    ymean = dd.groupby("year")["anom_s"].mean()
    _jan = {y: dd[(dd["year"] == y) & (dd["month"] == 1)].head(1) for y in years}

    def yseg(y, visible=True):
        # one year's line, coloured by its mean anomaly; append next year's
        # January as a bridge so consecutive segments join (no Dec->Jan gap)
        s = dd[dd["year"] == y]
        if y + 1 in _jan and len(_jan[y + 1]):
            s = pd.concat([s, _jan[y + 1]])
        return go.Scatterpolar(
            r=(s["anom_s"].round(2).tolist() if visible else []),
            theta=(s["theta"].round(1).tolist() if visible else []),
            mode="lines", line=dict(color=_col(float(ymean[y])), width=2),
            hoverinfo="skip")

    fyears = years[::3]
    if fyears and fyears[-1] != years[-1]:
        fyears.append(years[-1])

    # green "normal" ring at 0 °C (the 1961–1990 baseline) + a semi-opaque wedge behind
    # the radial °C labels (review §484) — both kept AFTER the year traces so the
    # year-only frames (which address traces 0..N-1) never overwrite them. The wedge sits
    # over the lines so the ticks read (radial axis at angle=0 → horizontal labels on the
    # right spoke, off the January tick), while staying a touch transparent.
    _th = list(range(0, 361, 6))
    ring = go.Scatterpolar(r=[0] * len(_th), theta=_th, mode="lines",
                           line=dict(color="#2ca02c", width=1.5), hoverinfo="skip")
    wedge = go.Scatterpolar(
        r=[_RR[0], _RR[0], _RR[1], _RR[1]], theta=[79, 101, 101, 79],
        fill="toself", fillcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.82)", line=dict(width=0),
        mode="lines", hoverinfo="skip")
    fig = go.Figure(
        data=[yseg(y) for y in years] + [ring, wedge],       # year loops + baseline ring + label wedge
        frames=[go.Frame(name=str(Y), data=[yseg(y, visible=(y <= Y)) for y in years])
                for Y in fyears])
    fig.update_layout(
        height=440, showlegend=False, margin=dict(t=52, b=44, l=22, r=22),
        title=dict(text=title, x=0.5, xanchor="center", y=0.97, font=dict(size=13)),
        polar=dict(
            radialaxis=dict(range=_RR, tickvals=_TICKS, ticktext=_TTXT, angle=0,
                            layer="above traces", tickfont=dict(size=11, color="#1a1a1a"),
                            gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
            angularaxis=dict(direction="clockwise", rotation=90, gridcolor="#eeeeee",
                             tickfont=dict(size=8), tickmode="array", ticktext=MON_S,
                             tickvals=[(m - 1) / 12 * 360 for m in range(1, 13)])),
        updatemenus=[dict(type="buttons", direction="left", showactive=False,
            x=0.0, y=1.10, xanchor="left", yanchor="top", pad=dict(r=4, t=2),
            buttons=[
                dict(label="▶", method="animate",
                     args=[None, dict(frame=dict(duration=180, redraw=True), transition=dict(duration=0))]),
                dict(label="❚❚", method="animate",
                     args=[[None], dict(frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=False), mode="immediate")])])],
        sliders=[dict(active=len(fyears) - 1, x=0.05, len=0.9, y=-0.02, pad=dict(t=2),
            currentvalue=dict(prefix="", font=dict(size=11)),
            steps=[dict(method="animate", label=str(Y),
                args=[[str(Y)], dict(mode="immediate", frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                                     transition=dict(duration=0))]) for Y in fyears])])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "displayModeBar": False})
```

::: {layout-ncol=2}

```{python}
#| echo: false
show_spiral("Toronto", "Toronto · southern Canada")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
show_spiral("Eureka", "Eureka · High Arctic")
```

:::

*Same scale on both, 1948 onward (when the Arctic stations begin). Full monthly anomalies — no smoothing — from the homogenized record (to 2020); the green ring is the 1961–1990 normal; each loop is coloured by that year's average departure. Source: ECCC AHCCD, via the GeoMet API.*

## Download data

- [National temperature departure (CSV)](../data/environment/cesi_temperature.csv) — ECCC CESI (vs 1961–1990)
- [Regional warming trends (CSV)](../data/environment/cesi_ctvb_regional.csv) — ECCC Climate Trends and Variations Bulletin
- [Long-run city temperatures (CSV)](../data/environment/city_temperatures.csv) — ECCC AHCCD (homogenized) + MSC daily (raw), via GeoMet
- [Seasonal city temperatures (CSV)](../data/environment/city_temperatures_seasonal.csv) — ECCC AHCCD seasonal (homogenized), via GeoMet
- [Monthly city temperatures (CSV)](../data/environment/city_temperatures_monthly.csv) — ECCC AHCCD monthly (homogenized), via GeoMet

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## About these data

**Homogenized vs. raw.** A *homogenized* record (AHCCD) has been adjusted to remove
the effects of station relocations, instrument changes, and changes in observing
practice, so that the remaining year-to-year change reflects climate rather than the
measurement setup. It is the right series for trends, but ECCC's openly published
version currently ends in 2020. The *raw* recent tail is the unadjusted annual mean
from current daily observations — current to last year, but not directly comparable
to the homogenized series, which is why the two are drawn as separate lines.

**Baselines.** Departures are measured against the 1961–1990 average, the standard
reference period used by ECCC and the World Meteorological Organization. A positive
departure means warmer than that baseline.

**Arctic records.** The High Arctic has no instrumental record before the late
1940s; Alert (1950) and Eureka (1947) were among the first permanent stations, built
under the Joint Arctic Weather Stations program. All data are published under the
Open Government Licence – Canada.
:::
